title.en: Medical Latin — 3rd Declension Pure i-Stems: Core Mastery
title.de: Medizinisches Latein — Reine i-Stämme der 3. Deklination

description.en: Advanced morphology deck for first-year Medical Latin. Focuses on the pure i-stems of the 3rd declension, including identification criteria, paradigm structure, parisyllabic masculine and feminine nouns in -is, neuter nouns in -e, -al, and -ar, the genitive plural in -ium, and the historically preserved accusative singular -im and ablative singular -i. Designed to establish reliable recognition of pure i-stem patterns and to strengthen their use in medically relevant vocabulary and phrases.
description.de: Fortgeschrittenes Morphologie-Deck für Medizinisches Latein im ersten Studienjahr. Im Mittelpunkt stehen die reinen i-Stämme der 3. Deklination mit ihren Erkennungsmerkmalen, ihrer Paradigmenstruktur, den parisyllabischen maskulinen und femininen Substantiven auf -is, den Neutra auf -e, -al und -ar, dem Genitiv Plural auf -ium sowie dem historisch erhaltenen Akkusativ Singular auf -im und Ablativ Singular auf -i. Das Deck dient dem sicheren Erkennen reiner i-Stamm-Muster und ihrer Anwendung in medizinisch relevanter Terminologie und Wortgruppen.

question.en: Which two major groups belong to the Latin 3rd-declension pure i-stems?

cards.correctAnswer: Parisyllabic masculine/feminine nouns in -is, and neuter nouns in -e, -al, or -ar
options: Parisyllabic masculine/feminine nouns in -is, and neuter nouns in -e, -al, or -ar

explanation.en: Pure i-stems in this exam block are defined by two structural subgroups: masculine/feminine parisyllabic nouns with nominative singular in -is, and neuter nouns with nominative singular in -e, -al, or -ar. That split determines how you predict the paradigm.

question.en: What is the key plural ending that distinguishes pure i-stem masculine/feminine nouns from consonant stems?

explanation.en: The exam-critical differentiator is genitive plural -ium. Many other endings overlap with consonant stems, but -ium is the classic high-yield signal for the pure i-stem pattern.

question.en: In historical pure i-stem morphology, what was the older accusative singular ending of many masculine/feminine nouns?

explanation.en: Pure i-stems originally had accusative singular -im and ablative singular -i. In later usage, many masculine/feminine forms show replacement by the more common consonant-stem-like endings -em and -e.

question.en: In historical pure i-stem morphology, what was the older ablative singular ending of many masculine/feminine nouns?

explanation.en: The older pure i-stem ablative singular is -i. This matters because it explains why neuter pure i-stems also consistently preserve ablative singular -i.

question.en: What does 'parisyllabic' mean in this context?

cards.correctAnswer: The nominative singular and genitive singular have the same number of syllables
options: The word has only two syllables
The stem ends in a vowel
The nominative singular and genitive singular have the same number of syllables

explanation.en: Parisyllabic means equal in syllable count across nominative singular and genitive singular. This is a classic recognition tool for masculine/feminine pure i-stems.

question.en: Which nominative singular ending is expected for many masculine/feminine pure i-stem nouns in this deck?

explanation.en: In this block, masculine/feminine pure i-stems are typically listed as nominative singular in -is with genitive singular in -is.

question.en: Which set identifies neuter pure i-stem nominative singular endings?

explanation.en: The neuter pure i-stem subgroup is classically recognized by nominative singular endings -e, -al, and -ar. These forms have highly characteristic plural behavior.

question.en: What is the nominative plural of neuter pure i-stems?

explanation.en: Neuter pure i-stems take nominative plural -ia. The same applies to accusative plural because neuter nominative and accusative match.

question.en: What is the accusative plural of neuter pure i-stems?

explanation.en: Neuter rules apply: nominative plural and accusative plural are identical. In pure i-stems, the neuter plural marker is -ia.

question.en: Which ablative singular ending is consistently preserved in neuter pure i-stems?

explanation.en: Neuter pure i-stems preserve ablative singular -i. This is one of the most important endings to memorize together with plural -ia and genitive plural -ium.

question.en: Which ending pair best summarizes the core neuter pure i-stem plural signature?

explanation.en: For neuter pure i-stems, the exam signature is plural -ia plus genitive plural -ium. This combination separates them from many other declensional patterns.

question.en: For masculine/feminine pure i-stems, which paradigm row is most testable?

explanation.en: The highest-yield row is genitive plural -ium. Nominative plural -es and ablative plural -ibus are less distinctive because they overlap with other 3rd-declension patterns.

question.en: Which pair reflects the later replacement endings often seen instead of older pure i-stem -im and -i in masculine/feminine nouns?

explanation.en: Historically older -im and -i were often displaced in usage by -em and -e, which resemble ordinary 3rd-declension consonant-stem behavior.

question.en: Which form is the best exam cue that a noun like febris belongs to the pure i-stem group?

explanation.en: The strongest distinctive marker is the genitive plural in -ium: febrium. Other forms can overlap with consonant-stem patterns and are therefore less diagnostic.

question.en: Which statement about pure i-stems is most accurate?

cards.correctAnswer: Not every pure i-stem form looks unique; the genitive plural is often the decisive clue
options: Every case ending is unique to pure i-stems
Not every pure i-stem form looks unique; the genitive plural is often the decisive clue
They always keep -im and -i in all later medical Latin usage

explanation.en: This is the practical exam rule. Some forms overlap with ordinary 3rd declension, so you must rely on the most discriminating endings and the noun class itself.

question.en: How do you derive the stem of febris, is f?

cards.correctAnswer: Remove the genitive singular ending -is to get febr-
options: Remove the genitive singular ending -is to get febr-
Add -i to the nominative to get febri- only

explanation.en: The standard method is to use the genitive singular. From febris, is, you remove -is and obtain the stem febr-. Then you add case endings.

question.en: Which stem is correct for mare, is n?

explanation.en: The stem is derived from the genitive singular maris. Remove -is and you get mar-. Then build forms such as maria and marium.

question.en: Which complete paradigm fragment is correct for a neuter pure i-stem like mare?

cards.correctAnswer: mare, maris, mare, mari, maria, marium, maria, maribus
options: mare, maris, marim, mari, maria, marium, maria, maribus
mare, maris, mare, mari, maria, marium, maria, maribus
mare, maris, marem, mare, mares, marum, mares, মারibus
mare, maris, mare, mari, mara, মারorum, mara, maris

explanation.en: Neuter pure i-stems keep nominative and accusative singular equal, use ablative singular -i, nominative/accusative plural -ia, and genitive plural -ium.

question.en: Which case ending do both consonant stems and pure i-stems commonly share in the ablative plural?

explanation.en: Ablative plural -ibus is common across many 3rd-declension nouns. That is why it is not the key discriminating ending for pure i-stems.

question.en: Which singular ending pair is historically associated with pure i-stems?

explanation.en: This pair is the mechanistic signature of the old pure i-stem singular. Even when later forms level the pattern, this remains the rule you must understand.

question.en: What is febris, is f in English?

explanation.en: Febris means fever. It is a classic feminine 3rd-declension pure i-stem noun and is often used in medical phrases such as contra febrim.

question.en: Which genitive plural is correct for febris?

explanation.en: Pure i-stem feminine nouns take genitive plural -ium. Therefore febris gives febrium, not februm.

question.en: What is tussis, is f in English?

explanation.en: Tussis means cough. It is a feminine pure i-stem and appears in fixed expressions like contra tussim.

question.en: What is pertussis, is f in English?

explanation.en: Pertussis is the disease name pertussis, commonly known as whooping cough. Morphologically it follows the same feminine pure i-stem pattern.

question.en: What is pelvis, is f in English?

explanation.en: Pelvis is a feminine pure i-stem noun. It is foundational for anatomical phrases such as cavitas pelvis.
Cavitas pelvis means pelvic cavity. The governing noun is cavitas, and pelvis is in the genitive singular.

question.en: What is basis, is f in English?

explanation.en: Basis is a feminine pure i-stem noun meaning base or basis. It is frequent in anatomy: basis cerebri, basis cranii interna, basis pulmonis.

question.en: Which phrase means 'base of the brain'?

explanation.en: Basis cerebri is the standard phrase for base of the brain. Cerebri is the genitive singular of cerebrum.

question.en: Which phrase means 'internal surface of the cranial base'?

explanation.en: Basis cranii interna means internal cranial base surface. Here interna agrees with basis in gender, number, and case.

question.en: Which phrase means 'base of the lung'?

explanation.en: Basis pulmonis means base of the lung. Pulmonis is genitive singular.

question.en: What does the clinical suffix -iasis usually denote?

cards.correctAnswer: A condition or state, often an unhealthy one
options: A condition or state, often an unhealthy one

explanation.en: In medical terminology, -iasis denotes a condition or state, especially a pathological one. This is why terms like lithiasis describe disease states involving calculi.
Lithiasis refers to formation of calculi or stones. The stem lith- means stone or calculus.
Lith- or litho- means stone or calculus. This allows you to decode terms such as nephrolithiasis and urolithiasis.

cards.correctAnswer: Formation or presence of a salivary calculus
options: Formation or presence of a salivary calculus
Stone in the urinary bladder only

explanation.en: Sialolithiasis combines sialo- with lithiasis and refers to salivary stones.
Nephrolithiasis means stones in the kidney. Nephro- points to the kidney, and -lithiasis indicates stone formation/presence.

cards.correctAnswer: Presence of calculi in the urinary system
options: Presence of calculi in the urinary system

explanation.en: Urolithiasis refers to calculi within the urinary system. Uro- localizes the condition to the urinary tract.
Stasis means stoppage or stagnation. In medicine it often refers to impaired flow.

cards.correctAnswer: Arrest of bleeding; also arrest/stagnation of blood flow
options: Arrest of bleeding; also arrest/stagnation of blood flow

explanation.en: Hemostasis classically means arrest of bleeding, but the term also carries the sense of stoppage or stagnation of blood flow in a part.

cards.correctAnswer: Obstruction of the normal flow of lymph
options: Obstruction of the normal flow of lymph

explanation.en: Lymphostasis means impaired or obstructed lymph flow. Mechanistically it is another application of the stasis concept.

question.en: What is anamnesis, is f in clinical usage?

cards.correctAnswer: Medical history / recollection of the patient's past
options: Medical history / recollection of the patient's past

explanation.en: Anamnesis refers to recollection and in clinical language to the patient's history. It is a frequent medical term built on a feminine pure i-stem noun.

cards.correctAnswer: A constitutional or inborn disposition / state
options: A constitutional or inborn disposition / state
A blood clot in an artery

explanation.en: Diathesis denotes a constitutional tendency or disposition. It is often used in pathology and clinical descriptions of predisposition.
Diathesis cystica means cystic diathesis. The adjective cystica agrees with diathesis in the nominative singular feminine.
Emesis means vomiting. It appears in compounds such as hematemesis.
Hematemesis is vomiting blood. Distinguish it from hemoptysis, which is coughing/spitting up blood from the respiratory tract.

question.en: Which pair is the key clinical distinction?

cards.correctAnswer: Hematemesis = vomiting blood; hemoptysis = spitting/coughing blood from lungs/bronchi
options: Hematemesis = kidney stones; hemoptysis = urinary stones
Hematemesis = vomiting blood; hemoptysis = spitting/coughing blood from lungs/bronchi

explanation.en: This is a must-know medical differentiation. Hematemesis is GI blood via vomiting, whereas hemoptysis is blood from the lungs or bronchial tree expelled by coughing/spitting.
Genesis means origin or formation. It overlaps semantically with productive processes in medical terminology.
Osteogenesis is bone formation and development. The term is useful for linking Latin morphology to biological process vocabulary.

cards.correctAnswer: Formation and development of blood cells
options: Formation and development of blood cells

explanation.en: Hematopoiesis is the formation of blood cellular elements. It belongs with genesis-type process words.
Paresis refers to partial paralysis or weakness. It must be distinguished from paralysis, which implies more complete loss of voluntary movement.

question.en: What is the main clinical distinction between paresis and paralysis?

cards.correctAnswer: Paresis is partial weakness; paralysis is loss of voluntary movement
options: Paresis is partial weakness; paralysis is loss of voluntary movement
Paresis means fever; paralysis means cough
Paresis is kidney stone disease; paralysis is vomiting blood
They are identical in all clinical usage

explanation.en: This is a classic look-alike distinction. Paresis indicates incomplete motor loss, whereas paralysis refers to major or complete loss of voluntary motor function.

cards.correctAnswer: Turning point of a disease / crisis
options: Turning point of a disease / crisis

explanation.en: Crisis is a classic medical noun meaning a turning point or critical period in disease.
Crisis myasthenica means myasthenic crisis. The adjective myasthenica agrees with crisis.
Crisis gastrica means gastric crisis. The phrase is structurally useful for practicing agreement with a feminine pure i-stem noun.

cards.correctAnswer: Phthisis / wasting disease, especially historical tuberculosis usage
options: Phthisis / wasting disease, especially historical tuberculosis usage

explanation.en: Phthisis is a historical clinical term associated with wasting disease, often pulmonary tuberculosis in older medical language.
Dosis means dose. It is a feminine pure i-stem noun and enters common therapeutic phrases.
Dosis curativa means curative or therapeutic dose. Curativa agrees with dosis in gender, number, and case.

cards.correctAnswer: A chronic liver disease with fibrosis and structural distortion
options: A chronic liver disease with fibrosis and structural distortion

explanation.en: Cirrhosis is a chronic liver pathology marked by fibrosis and architectural change. It belongs to the large clinical group of feminine nouns in -osis.

cards.correctAnswer: Bluish discoloration due to reduced oxygenation
options: Bluish discoloration due to reduced oxygenation

explanation.en: Cyanosis refers to bluish discoloration, classically linked to deoxygenated hemoglobin and hypoxemia/hypoxia.

cards.correctAnswer: A tendinous sheet / flat tendon-like structure
options: A tendinous sheet / flat tendon-like structure

explanation.en: Aponeurosis is a tendon-like fibrous sheet. It is common in anatomy and belongs to the large feminine -osis group.

cards.correctAnswer: Identification of a disease or syndrome; the process of determining cause and nature of illness
options: Identification of a disease or syndrome; the process of determining cause and nature of illness

explanation.en: Diagnosis is both the result and the process of identifying disease. It is one of the most testable nouns in this deck.
Diagnosis differentialis means differential diagnosis, the process of distinguishing among diseases with similar presentations.
Diagnosis ex exclusione means diagnosis by exclusion. Clinically, it refers to arriving at a diagnosis by ruling out alternatives.

cards.correctAnswer: Downward displacement / prolapse of the stomach
options: Downward displacement / prolapse of the stomach

explanation.en: Gastroptosis means prolapse or downward displacement of the stomach. The suffix -ptosis denotes falling or prolapse.
Glossopyrosis refers to a burning tongue sensation. The element pyro- signals fire, heat, or burning.

question.en: What does pyr- / pyro- commonly suggest in medical vocabulary?

explanation.en: Pyr- or pyro- marks heat, fire, burning, or fever. It helps decode terms like glossopyrosis.
Hemarthrosis means blood within a joint space. The term combines hema- with arthr-.

cards.correctAnswer: Condition marked by excessive deposit of fat
options: Condition marked by excessive deposit of fat

explanation.en: Lipomatosis refers to excessive fatty deposition, often localized. It belongs to the pathological -osis vocabulary group.

cards.correctAnswer: Disease caused by a fungus / fungal disease
options: Disease caused by a fungus / fungal disease

explanation.en: Mycosis is fungal disease. It is a common clinical noun in the -osis family.

cards.correctAnswer: Physiologic normal death of cells or tissues associated with development, aging, or use
options: Physiologic normal death of cells or tissues associated with development, aging, or use

explanation.en: Necrobiosis describes physiological cell or tissue death in normal processes. It contrasts with necrosis, which is pathologic.

question.en: What is the key distinction between necrobiosis and necrosis?

cards.correctAnswer: Necrobiosis is physiologic/normal cell death; necrosis is pathologic tissue death
options: Necrobiosis is physiologic/normal cell death; necrosis is pathologic tissue death
Necrobiosis is kidney stone disease; necrosis is vomiting blood
Necrobiosis is fungal disease; necrosis is arterial disease
They are exact synonyms in pathology

explanation.en: This is a high-yield clinical distinction. Necrobiosis can be part of normal biological turnover, whereas necrosis is injury-related pathologic death.

cards.correctAnswer: Pathologic death of cells, tissues, or organs
options: Pathologic death of cells, tissues, or organs

explanation.en: Necrosis is pathologic tissue death due to injurious insults such as ischemia, toxins, trauma, infection, radiation, or burns.
Necrosis suppurativa means suppurative necrosis. Suppurativa agrees with necrosis, a feminine noun.

cards.correctAnswer: Hardening / induration of tissue or an organ
options: Hardening / induration of tissue or an organ

explanation.en: Sclerosis denotes hardening or induration. The root scler- signals hardness.
Scler- means hard or hardness. It is essential for decoding sclerosis and related terms.

cards.correctAnswer: Constriction or narrowing of a passage or orifice
options: Constriction or narrowing of a passage or orifice

explanation.en: Stenosis means narrowing. The stem sten- / steno- captures the idea of constriction.
Steno- signals narrowing or constriction and is central for interpreting stenosis.

cards.correctAnswer: Formation or presence of a blood clot within the vascular system
options: Formation or presence of a blood clot within the vascular system

explanation.en: Thrombosis refers to intravascular clot formation or presence. It is a core clinical pathology term.
Thrombosis coronaria means coronary thrombosis. Coronaria agrees with thrombosis.
Thrombosis cerebralis means cerebral thrombosis. Cerebralis is a 3rd-declension adjective agreeing with thrombosis.

cards.correctAnswer: Spitting/coughing blood derived from lungs or bronchial tubes
options: Spitting/coughing blood derived from lungs or bronchial tubes

explanation.en: Hemoptysis is blood expectorated from the respiratory tract. Distinguish it carefully from hematemesis.

cards.correctAnswer: Dissolution or destruction of red blood cells
options: Dissolution or destruction of red blood cells

explanation.en: Hemolysis is the breakdown or destruction of red blood cells. The element -lysis indicates dissolution or loosening.
The suffix -lysis denotes dissolution or breakdown. That semantic value explains hemolysis and related terms.
Paralysis refers to loss of voluntary movement, often due to neurologic injury, disease, toxins, or drugs.
Rete is a neuter pure i-stem noun meaning anatomical network, often of vessels or fibers.
Rete arteriosum means arteriolar or arterial network. Arteriosum is the agreeing neuter adjective.

question.en: Which phrase means 'rete of the ovary'?

explanation.en: Rete ovarii means rete of the ovary. Ovarii is genitive singular.
Mare is a classic textbook neuter pure i-stem. It is important mainly as a paradigm model rather than as a medical term.

question.en: Which nominative plural is correct for mare?

explanation.en: As a neuter pure i-stem, mare forms nominative plural maria, not mares.

question.en: Which genitive plural is correct for mare?

explanation.en: Neuter pure i-stems take genitive plural -ium, so mare gives marium.

cards.correctAnswer: Spur; a spur-like process or sharp projection
options: A spur; a spur-like process or sharp projection

explanation.en: Calcar is a neuter pure i-stem in -ar. In anatomy it denotes a spur-like process or projection.

cards.correctAnswer: Thenar / the fleshy base of the thumb
options: Thenar / the fleshy base of the thumb

explanation.en: Thenar is a neuter pure i-stem in -ar and refers to the thenar region, the fleshy base of the thumb.
Animal is a neuter pure i-stem in -al. It is another paradigm anchor for the -al group.

question.en: Which noun is a neuter pure i-stem in -e?

explanation.en: Rete belongs to the neuter pure i-stem subgroup ending in -e. Basis, dosis, and crisis are feminine nouns in -is.

question.en: Which noun is a neuter pure i-stem in -al?

explanation.en: Animal belongs to the -al subgroup. Calcar and thenar are -ar nouns, and pelvis is feminine in -is.

question.en: Which noun is a neuter pure i-stem in -ar?

explanation.en: Calcar is a neuter pure i-stem in -ar. This subgroup shares the same core neuter i-stem endings.

question.en: Which set contains only feminine pure i-stem nouns from this deck?

explanation.en: Febris, tussis, crisis, and dosis are all feminine nouns in the pure i-stem block. The other sets mix in neuters.

question.en: Which set contains only neuter pure i-stem nouns from this deck?

explanation.en: Rete, mare, animal, and calcar are neuter pure i-stems. Thenar is also neuter, but the fully correct set here is the first one.
Contra febrim means against fever. The accusative singular here reflects the governed object of the preposition contra.
Contra governs the accusative, hence contra tussim. This phrase is useful because it preserves the older pure i-stem accusative in -im.

question.en: Why is contra tussim especially important for pure i-stem study?

cards.correctAnswer: It preserves the classic pure i-stem accusative singular ending -im
options: It is a genitive plural example
It preserves the classic pure i-stem accusative singular ending -im
It shows a neuter plural in -ia
It proves the noun is 1st declension

explanation.en: Contra tussim is high yield because it visibly demonstrates the old accusative singular -im. That is exactly the kind of form examiners like to test.

question.en: Which sentence means 'Good diagnosis, good cure'?

explanation.en: Diagnosis bona, curatio bona literally states that good diagnosis leads to good treatment or cure. It is useful for remembering diagnosis as a feminine noun.

question.en: Which sentence means 'That which is well diagnosed can be well cured'?

options: Et monere, et moneri, proprium est verae amicitiae.

explanation.en: Bene diagnoscitur, bene curatur links diagnostic precision to therapeutic success and reinforces the centrality of diagnosis in clinical reasoning.

question.en: Which proverb includes the pure i-stem noun tussis?

explanation.en: Amor tussisque non celantur means 'Love, and a cough, are not concealed.' It is memorable and helps fix tussis in long-term recall.

question.en: In noun-adjective agreement, what must happen in a phrase like thrombosis coronaria?

cards.correctAnswer: The adjective must agree with the noun in gender, number, and case
options: The adjective must agree only in meaning
The adjective must always be neuter
The adjective must agree with the noun in gender, number, and case
The adjective must always be 3rd declension

explanation.en: Agreement is fundamental. In phrases such as thrombosis coronaria, diagnosis differentialis, and dosis curativa, the adjective must match the head noun morphologically.

question.en: What is the most exam-efficient summary of 3rd-declension pure i-stems?

cards.correctAnswer: Recognize the subgroup, derive the stem from the genitive, and prioritize -ium, -i, and neuter -ia
options: Memorize only English meanings and ignore endings
Recognize the subgroup, derive the stem from the genitive, and prioritize -ium, -i, and neuter -ia
Treat all 3rd-declension nouns as consonant stems

explanation.en: That is the operational exam strategy: identify whether the noun is m/f parisyllabic in -is or neuter in -e/-al/-ar, derive the stem from the genitive, and anchor the paradigm around the truly discriminating endings.
